unit.name: Gradient descent and stochastic minibatch training
unit.description: Turns gradients into iterative optimization algorithms and explains why minibatches lead to SGD.
unit.summary: The lecture clarifies that if a loss is non-differentiable across most of its domain, steepest-descent methods may not give meaningful directions toward a solution [ts=1953s]. Gradient descent computes gradients of W from the loss, data, and current weights, then updates weights by moving in the negative-gradient direction scaled by a learning rate or step size [ts=2074s]. A contour plot shows original W, the negative-gradient direction, and a fixed step-size arrow moving toward lower loss [ts=2098s]. Stopping can be based on a fixed number of iterations or a tolerance when the loss no longer decreases enough [ts=2145s]. Full gradient descent requires summing over the entire training set, which becomes expensive when N is large [ts=2195s]. Stochastic gradient descent approximates the full sum using a randomly sampled minibatch, such as 256 training examples, then applies the same update rule [ts=2215s]. The randomness of the minibatch is what makes the method stochastic [ts=2232s].

key_points.text: If a loss is largely non-differentiable, steepest descent may not point toward a useful solution.
Gradient descent repeatedly computes gradients and moves in the negative-gradient direction.
The update subtracts a learning-rate-scaled gradient from the current weights.
Training can stop after fixed iterations or when loss improvement falls below a tolerance.
SGD approximates the full dataset gradient using a randomly sampled minibatch.
A batch size of 256 means each step estimates the gradient from 256 examples.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient checking
kp_catalog.description: Central-difference finite-difference estimates provide a slow but useful way to verify that a manual backward implementation is correct. Finite-difference derivative approximations, exact calculus-based gradients, and implementation checks comparing the two.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient checking formalizes how central-difference finite-difference estimates provide a slow but useful way to verify that a manual backward implementation is correct, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and medium.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent
kp_catalog.description: Neural networks are trained by computing the gradient of an objective and updating parameters with stochastic gradient descent. Gradient-based optimization updates parameters by subtracting a scaled gradient, with the learning rate controlling step size.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent formalizes how neural networks are trained by computing the gradient of an objective and updating parameters with stochastic gradient descent, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
